metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md / design-doc section capturing the three novel architectural contracts: (1) registry_version now hashes the .rebar/ overlay, (2) disabled_builtins is a first-class signed manifest field with expand-contract semantics, and (3) stale-regver is a new compute_validity invalidation axis — so future contributors can safely evolve attest.py and the registry without breaking attestation integrity.
findings.finding: No ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc addition is mentioned for the three novel architectural decisions introduced: (1) registry_version now incorporates the .rebar/ overlay hash as an invalidation axis, (2) disabled_builtins becomes a first-class signed manifest field, and (3) regver staleness becomes a new compute_validity invalidation reason alongside material/head-sha/reopen. These are durable contracts that future contributors must understand to safely evolve attest.py and the registry.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (or a section in CLAUDE.md / attest.py module docstring) stating: (a) registry_version(repo_root) contract: hashes packaged routing + overlay when repo_root given, absent overlay = same hash as today; (b) manifest field contract: all new fields are additive/expand-contract (absent on old certs = default value, never a crash); (c) invalidation axes: the three reasons (material, head-sha/reopen, stale-regver) and their precedence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote the built-in-disable implementation scope (`effective_criteria` exclusion, `effective_routing` flagging, `_validate_routing_entry` accepting `disabled: bool`, `disabled_builtins(repo_root)` helper) from remediation run 2 into the canonical 'What' and 'Scope' sections, and add the affected registry file(s) to the Scope file list.
findings.finding: The main 'What' and 'Scope' sections assume the built-in-disable mechanism (`effective_criteria` exclusion, `effective_routing` flagging) already exists from ef7e, but remediation run 2 reveals it does not — 'This story adds disable'. An executor reading only the canonical What/Scope sections would not know to implement `registry.effective_criteria` exclusion or `effective_routing` flagging for `disabled: true`, and could deliver a manifest line that references disabled_builtins with no upstream mechanism to populate it.
findings.location: 'What' section (disabled_builtins field) vs 'No mass re-review + the built-in-disable mechanism (remediation, run 2)' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'Scope → Files': `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py` (effective_criteria, effective_routing, disabled_builtins helper, _validate_routing_entry). Add to 'What': 'An overlay `disabled: true` on a built-in id removes it from `effective_criteria(repo_root)` (so its finder never runs); `disabled_builtins(repo_root) -> list[str]` exposes the set; `_validate_routing_entry` accepts `disabled: bool` only on un-prefixed (built-in) ids.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test that feeds `compute_validity` a signed manifest with NO `regver:` line and asserts the result is `valid=False, reason='stale-regver'` (not an exception), directly verifying the expand-contract promise.
findings.finding: The `manifest_regver` missing-line → stale (expand-contract) path is stated as a behavioral requirement but no test is called out that specifically exercises an old-format cert (no `regver:` line) being classified as stale rather than crashing.
findings.location: Remediation run 1 ACs: "`compute_validity` … a missing regver line → stale (re-review)"
findings.suggested_fix: Add to remediation run 1 ACs: "A test feeds `compute_validity` a manifest without a `regver:` line and asserts `valid=False, reason='stale-regver'` with no exception raised."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (or implement) the backward-compat rollback story: confirm that old code reading a new-format cert (with `disabled_builtins:` line) fails gracefully as 'stale/invalid → re-review' rather than 'tampered', OR provide a version-gated manifest parser so old code ignores unknown lines before HMAC re-derivation.
findings.finding: The plan addresses forward-compat (new code reading old certs → stale, re-review) but is silent on the backward-compat rollback direction: if the deployment is rolled back AFTER new-format certs (carrying the `disabled_builtins:` manifest line) have been written, old code will re-derive the manifest WITHOUT that line and the HMAC will not match — making every new-format cert appear tampered/invalid rather than merely stale, and forcing re-review on rollback.
findings.location: Remediation run 1 — '`disabled_builtins` is a dedicated signed manifest line' / `build_manifest` / `verify_signature`
findings.suggested_fix: One concrete option: prefix the manifest with a `manifest_version: 2` line (expand-contract: old code sees unknown version → treats cert as stale, not tampered) and gate HMAC re-derivation on the version, so unknown future lines are safely ignored by older code. Alternatively, document explicitly that rollback will cause re-review churn and that this is acceptable operational behaviour.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the `compute_validity` fallback for an absent `disabled_builtins:` line: 'absent → treated as `[]` (no disabled built-ins at signing time) — NOT stale' and distinguish this from the regver-absent → stale rule.
findings.finding: The expand-contract fallback rules for the two new manifest fields are asymmetric and unresolved in the plan: `manifest_regver` returning None (absent line) → stale/re-review, but `manifest_disabled_builtins` returning `[]` (absent line) → treated as clean. An implementer reading AC-run-1-remediation and AC4 together cannot determine which fallback pattern applies to `disabled_builtins` on old certs.
findings.location: AC4 / Remediation run 1 AC2; 'absent/empty on a clean run (byte-identical manifest)' vs. 'old attestation lacking the new basis is treated as stale'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `_DISABLED_BUILTINS_PREFIX` constant, the exact line format (`disabled_builtins: <sorted-comma-separated-ids>`), and the `manifest_disabled_builtins` parser function in `attest.py` — co-locate with the existing `_REGVER_PREFIX` and `manifest_regver` pattern.
findings.finding: The `build_manifest` `disabled_builtins:` line format (prefix constant, serialisation format for the id list, and the `manifest_disabled_builtins` parser) is referenced throughout the plan but never defined in any live code or in the plan body.
findings.location: Remediation run 1 AC / `build_manifest` emits a deterministic `disabled_builtins: [ids]` line; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:151–192
findings.suggested_fix: Add `_DISABLED_BUILTINS_PREFIX = "disabled_builtins:"` in attest.py alongside `_REGVER_PREFIX`; emit `f"{_DISABLED_BUILTINS_PREFIX} {','.join(sorted(disabled))}"` in `build_manifest`; parse with a `manifest_disabled_builtins` function mirroring `manifest_regver`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `disabled_builtins(repo_root) -> list[str]` lives in `registry.py` (alongside `effective_criteria`) and define its interaction with `effective_routing` / `_load_overlay`.
findings.finding: The `disabled_builtins(repo_root) -> list[str]` helper is referenced as the exposure point for the disabled-builtin set, but no owning module is specified and the function does not exist in the live codebase.
findings.location: Remediation run 2 / 'A `disabled_builtins(repo_root)` helper lists them'; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `registry_version(repo_root)` incorporates the overlay by calling `registry._overlay_signature(repo_root)` and folding its return value into the `basis` dict (e.g. as `"overlay": _overlay_sig`), reusing the single path-resolution source.
findings.finding: The `registry_version(repo_root)` hashing strategy for the `.rebar/` overlay is unspecified relative to the already-existing `_overlay_signature(repo_root)` helper in registry.py — the plan says 'additionally hashes the overlay' but does not say whether `_overlay_signature` is called or the overlay bytes are independently re-hashed.
findings.location: What section / 'registry_version(repo_root) additionally hashes the `.rebar/` overlay'; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:37–57 and registry.py:182–192
findings.suggested_fix: In `attest.registry_version`, import and call `registry._overlay_signature(repo_root)` and add `"overlay": registry._overlay_signature(repo_root)` to the `basis` dict; this reuses the existing single-source path constant and avoids a separate file open.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify WHERE the '`project.` ids cannot carry `disabled: true`' enforcement lives: either (a) extend `_validate_routing_entry` to accept an `is_builtin: bool` flag and raise on `disabled: true` for non-built-ins, or (b) enforce it in the `_effective_routing_cached` call-site's per-`cid` dispatch (where `is_builtin` is already computed) — and state the chosen option explicitly so the validator's signature contract is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan states '`_validate_routing_entry` accepts `disabled: bool` only on an un-prefixed (built-in) id (a `project.` id cannot be "disabled")' but the existing `_validate_routing_entry(cid, entry, *, where)` does not receive `is_builtin` context — it validates field shapes only, with no knowledge of whether `cid` is in `CANONICAL_LLM`. The `is_builtin` branch is currently computed in `_effective_routing_cached` AFTER calling the validator. Enforcing 'only built-in ids may carry `disabled: true`' either requires (a) passing `is_builtin` into `_validate_routing_entry` or (b) checking for `disabled: true` on a `project.` id at the call site in `_effective_routing_cached`. The plan names `_validate_routing_entry` as the enforcement point but does not describe the required signature change — leaving an implementer uncertain whether to modify the validator's signature or the call site. A `project.foo` entry with `disabled: true` that slips through would cause `effective_criteria` to silently not-exclude (since `_exclude_disabled` logic only targets built-ins), corrupting coverage records.
findings.location: ## No mass re-review + the built-in-disable mechanism (remediation, run 2) — '`_validate_routing_entry` accepts `disabled: bool` only on an un-prefixed (built-in) id'; `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/registry.py:218-241`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly where in `_validate_routing_entry` (or in `_effective_routing_cached` post-validation) the `disabled: bool` field is checked and the rule 'reject on `project.*` id' is enforced — co-locate the rule with the schema that validates it.
findings.finding: `_validate_routing_entry` in registry.py (lines 218–241) does not validate a `disabled: bool` key; the plan introduces the rule that `disabled: true` is accepted only on un-prefixed built-in ids and rejected on `project.*` ids, but never specifies WHERE in the function the check is inserted or what fields it co-exists with.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `_validate_routing_entry`: after the `default_posture` check, if `entry.get('disabled')` is not None, assert it's a bool and assert the `cid` is NOT `project.`-prefixed (raise RegistryError if so). This keeps the rule in the same function that enforces all other structural constraints.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State that `registry_version(repo_root)` folds in the overlay by **reusing `registry._overlay_signature(repo_root)`** (the existing sha256-of-overlay-bytes helper) as the overlay component of the basis, rather than re-implementing the hash — and explain why (reusing the canonical overlay fingerprint avoids divergence from the cache-key contract).
findings.finding: The plan proposes that `registry_version(repo_root)` 'additionally hashes the `.rebar/` overlay' but gives no positive rationale for HOW — specifically, whether to reuse the already-present `registry._overlay_signature(repo_root)` (lines 182-192 of registry.py, which is sha256 of the overlay file bytes) or to re-implement the hash inline. Since `_overlay_signature` is the codebase's canonical overlay content fingerprint and is already tested, leaving this unspecified risks the implementer duplicating logic or producing a subtly different hash (e.g. hashing parsed JSON vs. raw bytes), which would break the cache-key contract the registry's LRU caches rely on.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add: 'Concretely, `registry_version(repo_root)` extends its basis dict with `"overlay": registry._overlay_signature(repo_root)` (returns `""` when absent, preserving the overlay-absent parity guarantee); this reuses the same sha256-of-raw-bytes logic the LRU cache keys use.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `registry_version(repo_root)` incorporates the overlay hash by calling/reusing `_overlay_signature(repo_root)` from `registry.py` (lines 182–192) rather than re-deriving it, to prevent divergent hashing semantics between the cache-key path and the attestation-version path.
findings.finding: The plan proposes that `registry_version(repo_root)` will 'additionally hash the `.rebar/` overlay' as a new implementation, but `_overlay_signature(repo_root)` already exists in `registry.py` (lines 182–192) and already computes `sha256(overlay-bytes)` — the exact same content hash the plan describes. The plan commits to an implementation path without referencing or reusing this directly applicable prior art, risking a duplicate/divergent hashing scheme (two separate sha256-of-overlay-bytes computations that could drift).
findings.suggested_fix: In the `registry_version(repo_root)` implementation, import and call `_overlay_signature(rr)` (the existing helper) as the overlay component of the hash basis, e.g. add `"overlay": _overlay_signature(rr)` to the `basis` dict. This reuses the already-validated, already-tested sha256(overlay-bytes) logic and keeps both paths in sync.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what 'flagged' means in `effective_routing` for a disabled entry (e.g., the routing dict entry retains its keys and gains `"disabled": true`, or a separate `_disabled_ids: set` is returned alongside the routing dict) so the executor does not need to invent the representation.
findings.finding: The plan states `effective_routing` 'retains the entry but flags' a disabled built-in, without specifying what 'flagged' means — the field name, type, or value used to mark the entry is undefined, leaving the executor to invent the representation.
findings.location: 'No mass re-review + the built-in-disable mechanism (remediation, run 2)' section: 'effective_routing (the entry is retained but flagged)'
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify as: 'effective_routing returns the routing dict with the disabled entry's value set to `{"disabled": true}` (all other keys stripped), consistent with how `_validate_routing_entry` accepts the field.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend remediation run-2 AC9) requiring a test that `_validate_routing_entry` rejects `disabled: true` on a `project.`-prefixed id with an appropriate error.
findings.finding: The `_validate_routing_entry` rule 'accepts `disabled: bool` only on an un-prefixed (built-in) id; a `project.` id cannot be disabled' is described in the body but has no covering acceptance criterion or test requirement anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: 'No mass re-review + the built-in-disable mechanism' section, body paragraph on `_validate_routing_entry`
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC9 with: 'A test asserts that an overlay entry with `disabled: true` on a `project.`-prefixed id raises a validation error (is rejected by `_validate_routing_entry`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider splitting the built-in-disable mechanism (effective_criteria filtering, disabled_builtins helper, _validate_routing_entry) into a thin preparatory story so the core attestation-invalidation story (registry_version overlay hash + compute_validity staleness check + threading) can ship and be validated independently.
findings.finding: The plan has 9 acceptance criteria across 3 remediation rounds (5 original + 2 run-1 + 2 run-2), touches at least 3 layers (registry, attestation/signing, gate-check), and introduces 3 new helper functions (`manifest_regver`, `manifest_disabled_builtins`, `disabled_builtins(repo_root)`). The run-2 addition of the built-in-disable mechanism is a structurally independent concern (a new registry behavior) joined to the attestation-invalidation story by a 'and record it in the manifest' link — it could ship as a separate story.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all three rounds) / 'No mass re-review + the built-in-disable mechanism (remediation, run 2)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test asserting that `_validate_routing_entry` raises (or returns a validation error) when `disabled: true` appears on a `project.`-prefixed id, and a complementary test that it is accepted on an un-prefixed built-in id.
findings.finding: The new `_validate_routing_entry` rule — rejecting `disabled: true` on a `project.`-prefixed id — is a new user-facing validation failure path with no named test anywhere in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: In the remediation-run-2 ACs, add: "A test asserts `_validate_routing_entry` rejects `disabled: true` on any `project.`-prefixed id with a clear validation error, and accepts it on a built-in (un-prefixed) id."

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the title (or add a one-line scope statement at the top) to reflect that blocking is ALREADY shipped and this story is about attestation invalidation + built-in-disable recording — not new blocking logic.
findings.finding: The title says 'Blocking policy + attestation integration' but the plan body explicitly disclaims blocking ('Blocking itself is NOT in scope — already shipped via threshold_for + kernel Pass-3'). The only new blocking-adjacent work (the built-in-disable mechanism) is added in run-2 remediation as a scope expansion, not as the primary intent. A reader following the title will expect new blocking logic; the body delivers attestation plumbing.
findings.location: Title / Scope section / 'No mass re-review + the built-in-disable mechanism (remediation, run 2)'
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to 'Attestation invalidation + disabled-builtins recording (registry_version overlay hash, manifest fields)' and open a separate note if blocking needs a follow-on ticket.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete, testable AC for the no-rebind invariant: specify the enforcement point (e.g., `_validate_routing_entry` raises on a `project.*` id that matches a built-in id) and require a test asserting that a `project.` overlay entry cannot shadow or replace a built-in criterion.
findings.finding: The AC 'A project criterion's verdict + coverage fold into the signed attestation; it can never rebind a built-in' contains an unverifiable sub-clause: 'it can never rebind a built-in' has no specified enforcement mechanism in the ACs and no associated test. The plan mentions namespace prefix (`project.`) as the guard in remediation run 2, but this is buried in prose, not tied to a test assertion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section, final bullet: 'A project criterion's verdict + coverage fold into the signed attestation; it can never rebind a built-in.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a body description and a test criterion for the 'can never rebind a built-in' constraint in AC5: specify WHERE the guard lives (e.g. `_validate_routing_entry` or `effective_criteria`), what it checks (project-prefixed id cannot shadow a built-in id in the manifest), and add an AC that asserts a project overlay entry with a built-in id is rejected or ignored.
findings.finding: AC5's 'it can never rebind a built-in' constraint has no corresponding described implementation mechanism or test criterion — it is an orphan criterion fragment with no covering body text explaining HOW the rebind is prevented or WHERE in the code it is enforced.
findings.location: Initial Acceptance Criteria, AC5: 'A project criterion's verdict + coverage fold into the signed attestation; it can never rebind a built-in.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
